parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to just let the constant demands roll right off my shoulders without a second thought. Things that used to not bother me at all now make my chest tighten instantly. Back then, a heavy load felt like a normal part of the routine, and I could easily push through it. Now, even a minor request makes me want to shut everything down. I catch myself staring at the same screen for way too long, trying to muster up the energy to just hit reply. It is frustrating how quickly my patience has completely vanished.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really exhausting — like your capacity has been getting chipped away, and now even small things feel like too much. When patience and energy drop like that, it can make everything feel urgent and irritating, even the stuff you normally handle without thinking.

What you’re describing can happen when someone’s been under sustained stress, burned out, or just emotionally overloaded for a while. It doesn’t mean you’ve become lazy or weaker. It usually means your system is trying to protect itself by shutting down a bit.

A few things that might help right now:

- **Reduce the “entry cost” of tasks.** For example, reply with a one-line placeholder like:  
  *“Got this — I’ll respond in a bit.”*
- **Break the freeze into the smallest possible step.** Not “answer the email,” just “open the draft.”
- **Give yourself permission to be less available.** Constant responsiveness can drain you fast.
- **Notice whether there’s a bigger load underneath.** Sleep, stress, resentment, grief, burnout, or too many obligations can show up exactly like this.

If you want, I can help you sort through whether this feels more like burnout, anxiety, depression, or just being overloaded — or help you draft a reply to the screen-staring message you’ve been avoiding.

turns.user_message: I really wish I could go back to how I used to handle everything so effortlessly. A few months ago, I would just tackle the backlog and move on without dwelling on it. Lately, the same pile of tasks just sits there mocking me while I scroll aimlessly. I keep telling myself I will dive in tomorrow, but the dread just builds up overnight instead. It is exhausting to feel this heavy resistance every single morning when I know I should just get started.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really heavy, and honestly pretty painful. When something that used to feel straightforward suddenly turns into this wall of dread, it can make you feel stuck and frustrated with yourself at the same time.

What you’re describing is a really common loop:  
**task pile → dread → avoidance/scrolling → temporary relief → more dread later**.  
That doesn’t mean you’ve become lazy or incapable. It usually means something has shifted in your stress level, energy, confidence, or emotional bandwidth.

A few things may help break the cycle a little:

- **Make the first step tiny.** Not “work on the backlog,” but “open the file,” “write one sentence,” or “sort one item.”
- **Lower the bar for starting.** The goal is not to finish—just to begin for 5 minutes.
- **Name the resistance.** Sometimes saying, “I’m avoiding because this feels overwhelming,” reduces its power a bit.
- **Use a no-negotiation start ritual.** Same time, same place, same first action every day.
- **Stop asking tomorrow-you to save today-you.** Tomorrow often feels easier only because it’s not here yet.
- **Be gentle about the scrolling.** It’s often a coping move, not a moral failure.

If this has been going on for a while, it might also be worth asking:  
- Am I burned out?  
- Am I dealing with anxiety or low mood?  
- Have my expectations gotten too high?  
- Is the backlog emotionally loaded in some way?

If you want, I can help you turn one of those tasks into a very small, low-dread first step right now.

turns.user_message: I honestly do not recognize the person I used to be when I could power through anything without a second thought. Those days of bouncing back from a tough week feel like they belong to someone else entirely now. Every new notification sends a sharp wave of irritation straight through my chest, and I just want to close my eyes and pretend it is not there. I sit at my desk completely paralyzed while the hours slip away, hating how my own mind keeps shutting down on me. The sheer weight of it all has completely drained whatever spark I had left.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly heavy, and I’m really sorry you’re carrying this much right now. What you’re describing—feeling unlike yourself, getting overwhelmed by even small things, and going into a kind of freeze or shutdown—can be a sign that your mind and body are stretched past their limit. It doesn’t mean you’re weak or failing.

A few things I want to say clearly:

- You’re not lazy for being paralyzed at your desk.
- You’re not broken for not bouncing back the way you used to.
- This level of exhaustion and irritability is real, and it matters.

If it helps, try not to think about “fixing” everything today. Aim for one small stabilizing step:
- put the phone on Do Not Disturb for 20–30 minutes,
- stand up and get a glass of water,
- take 5 slow breaths,
- or pick just one tiny task that takes under 2 minutes.

If this has been going on for a while, or it’s getting in the way of work and daily life, it may really be worth talking to a therapist, doctor, or another mental health professional. Burnout, depression, anxiety, or chronic stress can look a lot like what you’re describing, and you deserve support with it.

If you want, I can help you sort through whether this feels more like burnout, depression, or overwhelm—or help you make a very small plan for getting through today.
